Estás en un directorio que contiene una tarea de programación. Lee los archivos disponibles y resuelve la tarea de la forma más completa posible.

Al terminar, deja instrucciones claras para ejecutar o revisar tu solución.

No hagas preguntas de seguimiento. Adopta supuestos razonables y documéntalos.

CC4302 Sistemas Operativos - Tarea 5

utilizando

En las siguientes 2 tareas Ud. debe implementar nuevas
herramientas
la
resolver
implementación de un sistema de subastas multi-threaded.
A continuación los encabezados de las funciones que debe
implementar

nThreads

para

enum { FALSE= 0, TRUE= 1 };
typedef struct subasta *nSubasta;

nSubasta *nNuevaSubasta(int n);
void nDestruirSubasta(nSubasta s);
double nAdjudicar( nSubasta s, int *punid   );
int nOfrecer(nSubasta s, double oferta,
             int timeout);

La función nNuevaSubasta inicializa la subasta por un
producto del cual se subastarán n items. Esta función es
invocada por el propietario de la subasta.

La función nDestruirSubasta se utiliza para liberar los recursos
solicitados para realizar la subasta.

La subasta se da por cerrada cuando el propietario decide
cerrarla invocando nAdjudicar.

Cuando el propietario cierra la subasta mediante la invocación
de la función nAdjudicar,
la cual retorna el monto total
recaudado y en la variable punid las unidades que no fueron
vendidas ya que llegaron menos oferentes que los n items
ofrecidos.

Múltiples oferentes interesados en la compra del producto,
realizan una oferta en dinero por un item del producto mediante
el llamado de la función nOfrecer y esperan una respuesta por
tiempo timeout.

La función nOfrecer queda a la espera hasta que:

i) La subasta se cierra, retornando TRUE cuando se adjudica un
producto y FALSE cuando se presentaron n oferentes con un
precio mayor a la oferta realizada.

Requerimientos:

la funciones pedidas y la
• Ud. debe programar
estructura del
tipo subasta en el archivo subasta.c,
utilizando las funciones nativas de nThreads, es decir
START_CRITICAL,
como
utilizando
setReady, suspend, schedule, etc.

operaciones

no

puede

de
sincronización pre-existentes a nThreads ( semáforos,
mutex, condiciones o mensajes).

herramientas

utilizar

las

TIP:

• Ud puede encontrar ejemplos de sincronización en
nThreads en el directorio nKernel, en los archivos sem.c,
mutex-cond.c y nmsgs.c.

• El siguiente mensaje de advertencia no es un problema,
support
==4952==WARNING:
makecontext/swapcontext functions and may produce false
positives in some cases!

doesn't

ASan

fully

T4: Implemente las funciones requeridas considerando el
parametro timeout = -1, es decir tiempo infinito.

T5: Implemente las funciones requeridas considerando
que existe un timeout y el nthread puede realizar una
nueva oferta.

Instrucciones

Use los archivos incluidos en este directorio. Ejecute el
comando make sin parámetros en el directorio T5 para
recibir instrucciones acerca del archivo en donde debe
programar su solución (T5/subasta.c), como compilar y
probar su solución, los requisitos que debe cumplir para
aprobar la tarea.

ii) Si se produce el timeout el nthread retorna FALSE.

La solución debe quedar en subasta.c y pasar los comandos de prueba indicados.

Observe que al momento que T4 oferta un precio de 5, existen 4
oferentes a la espera, siendo la oferta de T2(3) la peor por lo que
pierde y nOferta retorna FALSE. T2 realiza una nueva oferta lo
que produce que a T3(4) se le retorna FALSE.

Cuando T5 invoca nAdjudicar se le retorna 18 y u=0, pues no
quedan item sin vender. Los ganadores adjudicados fueron
T1(7), T2(6) y T4(5).

==========================================================
Esta es la documentación para compilar y ejecutar su tarea
==========================================================

Se está ejecutando el comando: less README.txt

***************************
*** Para salir: tecla q ***
***************************

Para avanzar a una nueva página: tecla <page down>
Para retroceder a la página anterior: tecla <page up>
Para avanzar una sola línea: tecla <enter>
Para buscar un texto: tecla / seguido del texto (/...texto...)
         por ejemplo: /ddd

-----------------------------------------------

Ud. debe programar las funciones solicitadas en el archivo subasta.c.

Pruebe su tarea bajo Debian 12 de 64 bits.  Estos son los requerimientos
para aprobar su tarea:

+ make run-san debe felicitarlo y no reportar ningún incidente en el
  manejo de memoria.

+ make run debe felicitarlo por aprobar este modo de ejecución.

+ make run-g debe felicitarlo.

Cuando pruebe su tarea con make run en su computador asegúrese de que
está configurado en modo alto rendimiento y que no estén corriendo
otros procesos intensivos en uso de CPU al mismo tiempo.

Invoque el comando make zip para ejecutar todos los tests y generar un
archivo subasta.zip que contiene subasta.c, con su solución, y resultados.txt,
con la salida de make run, make run-g y make run-san.

Para depurar use: make ddd

Video con ejemplos de uso de ddd: https://youtu.be/FtHZy7UkTT4

Ejecute make zip si necesita generar resultados.txt junto con la solución.

-----------------------------------------------

Limpieza de archivos

make clean

Hace limpieza borrando todos los archivos que se pueden volver
a reconstruir a partir de los fuentes: *.o binarios etc.

-----------------------------------------------

Acerca del comando make

El comando make sirve para automatizar el proceso de compilación asegurando
recompilar el archivo binario ejecutable cuando cambió uno de los archivos
fuentes de los cuales depende.

A veces es útil usar make con la opción -n para que solo muestre
exactamente qué comandos va a ejecutar, sin ejecutarlos de verdad.
Por ejemplo:

   make -n ddd

También es útil usar make con la opción -B para forzar la recompilación
de los fuentes a pesar de que no han cambiado desde la última compilación.
Por ejemplo:

   make -B run

content.text:
**Planning auction implementation**

I need to implement an auction system that includes a waiting queue and a timeout feature. It looks like I should develop a custom time queue to manage deadlines using only nthreads primitives. I want to infer the necessary semantics from tests, specifically understanding tasks T4 (which has no timeout) and T5 (which includes a timeout and re-offer). I see the initial file, subasta.c, is blank, so I probably need to compile it against NTHREADS. I’ll check the Makefile to confirm.

turns.toolResult:
# Para revisar las opciones de compilacion y ejecucion,
# ingrese en el terminal el comando: make
#
#

PROB=subasta

ARCH=$(shell arch)

NTHSRC=nKernel/nthread.c nKernel/nutil.c nKernel/ntime.c nKernel/pss.c \
       nKernel/sched-fcfs.c nKernel/sched-rr.c \
       nKernel/sched-pri-1core.c nKernel/sched-fcfs-1core.c \
       nKernel/sem.c nKernel/mutex-cond.c nKernel/nmsgs.c \
       nKernel/nsystem.c

NTHHDR=nKernel/nthread.h nKernel/nthread-impl.h nKernel/pss.h

SRCS= test-$(PROB).c $(PROB).c $(NTHSRC)
HDRS= $(PROB).h $(NTHHDR)

#DEFS=-DNTHSTOP -DTRACESCHED
DEFS=

SHELL=bash -o pipefail

INCLUDE=-InKernel
CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -DNTHREADS -std=c18 $(INCLUDE) $(DEFS)
#CFLAGS=-Wall -Werror -pedantic -std=c18 $(INCLUDE)
LDLIBS=-lm -lpthread -lrt

MAK=make --no-print-directory

readme:
	@less README.txt

$(PROB).bin $(PROB).bin-g $(PROB).bin-san $(PROB).bin-thr: $(SRCS) $(HDRS)

run-san: $(PROB).bin-san
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@echo --------------------------------------------
	./$(PROB).bin-san

run-thr: $(PROB).bin-thr
	@echo "No tiene sentido ejecutar sanitize para threads"
	@echo "porque no esta diseñado para nThread"
	@echo "No se extrañe por los errores que detecta"
	./$(PROB).bin-thr

run-g: $(PROB).bin-g
	@echo --------------------------------------------
	./$(PROB).bin-g  
	@echo --------------------------------------------
	./$(PROB).bin-g -slice 1 -ncores 4

run: $(PROB).bin
	@echo --------------------------------------------
	./$(PROB).bin -ncores 4

run-par: $(PROB).bin
	@echo --------------------------------------------
	./$(PROB).bin -ncores 4
	@echo --------------------------------------------
	./$(PROB).bin -slice 1 -ncores 4

ddd: $(PROB).ddd

ddd-san: $(PROB).ddd-san

zip:
	@if grep -P '\t' $(PROB).c ; then echo "Su archivo $(PROB).c contiene tabs.  Reemplacelos por espacios en blanco!" ; false ; else true; fi
	@rm -f resultados.txt $(PROB).zip
	@echo "Sistema operativo utilizado" > resultados.txt
	@uname -a >> resultados.txt
	@cat resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-san ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-san | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run-g ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run-g | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== run ==== | tee -a resultados.txt
	@$(MAK) -B run | tee -a resultados.txt
	@echo ==== zip ====
	zip -r $(PROB).zip resultados.txt $(PROB).c
	@echo "Archivo $(PROB).zip generado"
	@echo "Revise que contiene su ultima version de $(PROB).c"

%.bin: %.c
	gcc -g -O -DOPT=1 $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-g: %.c
	gcc -g $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-thr: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address $(CFLAGS) $(INCLUDE) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.bin-san: %.c
	gcc -g -DSAN=1 -fsanitize=address -fsanitize=undefined $(CFLAGS) $(SRCS) $(LDLIBS) -o $@

%.ddd: %.bin-g
	ddd $(*F).bin-g &

%.ddd-san: %.bin-san
	ddd $(*F).bin-san &

clean:
	rm -rf *.o *.log *.bin* core bin obj resultados.txt $(PROB).zip

content.text:
**Considering auction design**

I need to inspect the README to devise a solid auction design. The idea is to have a system where we choose the highest offers until all units are sold. A bidder would have to wait until the auction closes or times out. If the number of bidders exceeds the items, some might lose out if they get outbid. The function nOfrecer should return FALSE if a bid is lower than the current top offers, but only after waiting for closing or timeout. It returns TRUE when the auction ends and the bid is winning.

**Evaluating auction offer logic**

If there are more than n offers, I can reject low offers early. Bidders should be able to re-offer after losing or timing out, treating it as a new bid. I need to ensure the same thread can call nOfrecer again after a FALSE return, but concurrent offers from the same thread should likely be restricted. To maintain fairness, I might need to implement priority among equal-price offers. For example, if T2 makes a new offer and causes T3 to return FALSE, we should determine the lowest among existing bidders. A FIFO system might work best for ties at the same price.

**Creating a priority queue**

I need to implement a stable priority queue based on price and arrival order, making sure not to use external synchronization primitives. I must work with nthread functions and my own structures since the nthread scheduler relies on critical sections. So, I'll utilize functions like START_CRITICAL, suspend, schedule, and setReady. For timeouts, I can use nth_programTimer, which is available in ntime.c for timing functions. However, it's not in subasta.h, so I’ll need to include nthread-impl.h for that.

**Evaluating auction mechanics**

I'm looking into how `nth_wakeThreads` interacts with `wakeUpFun` and the auction queue. It seems like `wakeUpFun` could help manage the queue by waking others or marking nodes. But if a bidder times out, I wonder if their exit allows lower offers to step in as winners. The prompt mentions that if a thread times out, it returns FALSE but doesn't clarify about allowing lower bids to come back. That aspect needs more thought!

**Examining auction dynamics**

I’m thinking about how a timeout in a thread leads to it giving up and returning false, but that doesn’t explicitly state that the auction state should adjust winners immediately. The auction needs to keep the top active offers. If a winner leaves, I wonder if the next best offer should take their place. If the owner hasn't closed the auction, the remaining top offers are still valid. It makes me think about how the system should handle situations when offers timeout or are outbid.

**Understanding bidding dynamics**

I’m realizing that a bidder can't really be outbid individually since each offer is for a single item. Instead, new bids need to displace the worst among the current top offers, which then returns false. This means the top offers can change dynamically. If a winning bidder times out, the next best offer should be promoted. We need to maintain a sorted list of active offers and make sure that only the top offers stay as potential winners. But I wonder what happens if a waiting winner times out or is rejected.

**Exploring auction threads**

I'm working through some auction mechanics. The thread might handle multiple auctions, but we can only have one active offer at a time. I’m considering utilizing `thisTh->ptr`, but I'm not sure if it's reserved or can be used—probably best to avoid that. I wonder if I can store the node pointer in the thread’s `send.msg`, but that doesn't seem right. Instead, the node struct could contain `nThread th`, making it easier to find specific nodes when new offers come in. Keeping a tail pointer should help maintain a sorted list.

**Clarifying timeout values**

I’m trying to figure out the timeout values in this code. The tests seem to use `timeout*1000` to convert milliseconds to microseconds. In the function call `crearAgente`, the timeout looks like it’s in milliseconds, but then it gets multiplied by 1000 inside the agent. So, is the `nOfrecer` function expecting microseconds? The default timeout is `300000 ms`, but it seems like there’s a mix-up about whether it should be in microseconds or milliseconds. I need to look closely at how things are being called.

**Considering threading implementation**

I’m looking at the `nth_programTimer`, which takes a function pointer for any thread. I can set `thisTh->wakeUpFun` to `subasta_timeoutWake` and program the timer. The function only gets `nThread th`, and I might need to locate the bid node through `th->ptr`. It seems like I could use the `void *ptr` field that’s currently unused to store the node pointer. Since we're focusing on subasta, it should be fine to use `thread->ptr` for the current bid node.

content.text:
**Evaluating FIFO insertion logic**

I need to ensure that when managing FIFO among nodes of the same price, new nodes should go after the existing ones with equal prices. So I can traverse while the current node's price is greater than or equal to the new price, inserting after the last existing node with that price. If the list is full and the new price equals existing ones, it'll likely end up near the tail, which is acceptable. I'll make sure to keep sequence order stable during inserts.

**Clarifying insertion conditions**

I need to traverse the list while the current offer is greater than or equal to the new offer. If the list starts with prices all lower than the new one, I should insert at the head. When the new offer equals existing ones and the list is full, I'll insert at the tail and remove the previous tail. I think `nNuevaSubasta` should handle allocating and initializing the list, potentially using `nMalloc` or `malloc`, especially under thread-safe conditions. Including `stdlib.h` via `nthread-impl.h` seems like a good idea.

**Evaluating pointer issues**

I need to check if `nNuevaSubasta` returns the `nSubasta` type, not a pointer to a pointer, as there's a typo in the task markdown. I might need to mention this as an assumption. There could be an issue with `nDestruirSubasta` being called while there are still waiting threads. It seems like I need to handle any leftover threads properly. Also, if pending nodes exist, this could indicate user error. I guess we can just use `free` safely after verifying there are no pending nodes.

**Considering timeout behavior**

I’m considering the use of `fatalError`, which could terminate on an invalid state. It seems I should ensure that `nOfrecer` properly handles timeouts, setting immediate false if `timeout==0`. I can use `if (timeout==0) return FALSE;` or for infinite delays, see if `timeout<0`. Since `nThreads` uses `nth_programTimer(nanos<=0)`, it quickly sets `setReady(thisTh)`, meaning a `nSleepNanos` with 0 won’t block. I guess if `timeout==0`, it should return false right away for bids since no waiting is allowed.

**Deciding on timeout logic**

In `nOfrecer`, if `timeout==0`, I think I should check if the auction is already closed before detaching and returning FALSE. If it's accepted, then it seems reasonable to consider it an instant timeout. Implementing it as an immediate timeout after insertion could lead to issues like displacing a lower offer, which might let an unexpected lower offer be accepted. Since `timeout==0` means no waiting, it's probably best to refuse to wait and skip entering the waiting queue altogether.

**Evaluating revenue calculations**

I need to figure out how to handle revenue and unsold items. I’m cautious because `nAdjudicar` returns total recaudado and punid, and I think `revenue` might be double what it should be. There’s a conflict in the prompt saying it's double, but then tests seem to treat it as an integer. I’ll have to process `recaud = nAdjudicar(...)` carefully to avoid discrepancies! Let’s ensure calculations stay consistent.

**Examining bid design logic**

I’m considering our approach to handling bids. If we select a stable option, new bids that come in at an equal price shouldn’t push out existing ones if they were added later— that's acceptable. I think we should return FALSE for previously waiting bids when a new higher bid arrives. Only the top n bids are active; if more come in, we reject the lowest immediately. I also wonder about timing when suspending threads. Should I manage timers differently?

**Clarifying thread handling**

If the current thread is rejected, I notice there won’t be a `setReady` call, which seems fine for now. I might need to use `nSelf()` to access the current thread before calling `suspend`. Since the thread object stays intact across shifts, I should store the node pointer in `thisTh->ptr`. If the thread times out or gets rejected and returns false, clearing `thisTh->ptr=NULL` before freeing the node is essential. I think `wakeUpFun` may reference `th->ptr` to retrieve the node.

**Evaluating functions for thread cancellation**

I’m considering how to cancel a timer from a thread. It seems `nth_cancelThread` is the only public function available, but we could also use `nth_delTimed`. Although the latter is an internal function within `nthread-impl.h`, I think it’s permissible to call it. However, calling `nth_cancelThread` directly as intended might be simpler. If inside `nAdjudicar`, I worry about waking timed-out threads in the critical section. To prevent issues, I could gather the next pointer first and detach the node before calling `nth_cancelThread`.

**Analyzing thread cancellation impact**

I'm looking at how `nth_cancelThread` only affects the time queue and not my list directly, unless through `nth_wakeThreads`. It's possible that calling timeouts on other threads could lead their callbacks to detach themselves from my list. When iterating with `for (b=head; b!=NULL; b=next)`, I plan to store the next pointer ahead of time. By doing this, even if another callback removes `next` from the list, I won’t run into issues since I referenced the original list.

**Refining thread scheduling**

I'm thinking about simplifying this process for each woken thread. First, I'll set it to ready and then schedule it, continuing the loop from there. Since we're still in a critical section, it's possible that the schedule might return the same thread if there isn’t a higher ready thread. If a thread is awakened, it may run concurrently on another core, which is intended. But if `setReady` is called on a waiting thread with a timeout, I need to account for a potential timer signal that could be canceled.

**Implementing auction logic**

I’m thinking about how to handle rejections near the timeout expiry. If a rejection occurs just then, I can cancel the timer to clarify which outcome wins. It seems wise to introduce `nAdjudicar` if the list isn't empty and some threads have infinite timeouts but are closed already. If the auction's closed and there are no pending nodes, I might want to return zero for unsold units. Now it’s time to implement this in `subasta.c`. I should add comments and check my assumptions—keeping careful coding practices in mind!

content.text:
**Examining thread scheduling**

After coming back from a schedule, I'm thinking about suspending and programming a timer. I wonder if the current thread could have executed other code? Probably not, because the schedule returns later. It's somewhat like nSemPost. If setReady(perdedor) is scheduled and it has higher priority, it may run before the current thread suspends. I’m also considering the implications of context switching during critical sections and how to handle potential recursion with nOfrecer within the same auction. So many possibilities!

**Understanding thread critical sections**

I’m realizing that `schedule` can switch to another thread on a different core, but if the current thread is paused with a critical section count above zero, that new core likely can’t enter the critical section. This makes sense, as `nth_startCritical` grabs the scheduler lock when the critical level goes to one. Since the current thread is in a critical section, no other thread can change shared state until it exits, preventing any concurrency issues. I think I’ll need `nAdjudicar` to manage multiple threads while using setReady and schedule in between.

**Evaluating bid acceptance rules**

If a timeout occurs for a winner, only two waiters might remain. If a new bidder arrives after timeout, that's acceptable since there are only two waiters for three units. It seems plausible that our code will permit this because the pending bids are fewer than available units. I also need to consider if a lower bid can be accepted after a timeout if earlier rejected bids are no longer relevant. Could hidden tests check for this scenario?

**Patching memory checks**

I think in programming tasks, there's often an expectation for robust error handling. Maybe it would be wise to add checks like `if (s==NULL) nFatalError("nNuevaSubasta", "Not enough memory\n");` to prevent segmentation faults when using `malloc`. I should also ensure that `nDestruirSubasta` properly frees memory after the critical section is started. Additionally, I need to consider checking the allocation of `oferta`. Let’s make those patches small and effective!

I need to check if there are any more issues. I could set `s->recaudado` as unused, but it seems fine. I'll probably run `make -B run-san` quickly to ensure everything still passes after the patch. It’s likely all good, but just to be safe, I might do a compile and one run since it was already tested before the patch. The patch only added checks, so I think I’ll use `make run-san`.

content.text:
**Considering code execution and assumptions**

I need to provide clear instructions related to running the program, including mentioning any assumptions involved. It might be beneficial to note that `timeout` is measured in milliseconds, given that tests require it. Also, user instructions like `make run`, `make run-g`, and `make run-san` should be included.

I'll check if `nNuevaSubasta` uses only `nthread` primitives without mutex and ensure the `malloc` function's critical section is handled properly. If `malloc` fails and triggers a fatal error, it might cause issues with nesting critical sections. Therefore, avoiding `nFatalError` for Out Of Memory conditions might be best.

**Analyzing potential issues in timeout logic**

I see a potential issue with the way `timeout_subasta` interacts with `oferta_detach`, especially regarding the `pendientes` updates. If `timeout_subasta` runs before `setReady` and a node times out, there's a risk when `nAdjudicar` closes concurrently. I'll need to consider if `oferta_detach` might be called on the same node twice. If the timeout callback detaches a node, it won't be linked, so `nAdjudicar` won't find it.

Since the task mentions T4 and T5, it could involve hidden tests with and without timeouts. Our code functions correctly, so I should provide a concise final answer in Spanish, outlining assumptions and run instructions, perhaps as a bullet list.

Listo: implementé la subasta multithreaded en `subasta.c`.

Qué hace:
- admite ofertas concurrentes con ranking por precio
- despierta al perder por mejores ofertas
- soporta `timeout` finito e infinito
- permite re-ofertar tras perder o expirar
- cierra la subasta con `nAdjudicar` y libera recursos con `nDestruirSubasta`

Supuesto usado:
- `timeout` se interpreta en milisegundos, como lo hacen los tests.

Verificación:
- `make run-san` ✅
- `make run` ✅

Cómo ejecutar:
- `make run`
- `make run-g`
- `make run-san`

Archivo modificado:
- `subasta.c`
